q: The number of isomers possible for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>Pt</mi><mfenced><mi>en</mi></mfenced><msub><mfenced><msub><mi>NO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is:
question: The number of isomers possible for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>Pt</mi><mfenced><mi>en</mi></mfenced><msub><mfenced><msub><mi>NO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is:
solution:
PtenNO22

So, total possible isomers are 3.

The existence of coordination compounds with the same formula but different arrangements of the ligands was crucial in the development of coordination chemistry. Two or more compounds with the same formula but different arrangements of the atoms are called isomers.

q: The calculated magnetic moments (spin only value) for species <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><msub><mi>FeCl</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>Co</mi><msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mn>3</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mi>MnO</mi><mn>4</mn><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msubsup></math> respectively are:
question: The calculated magnetic moments (spin only value) for species <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><msub><mi>FeCl</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>Co</mi><msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mn>3</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mi>MnO</mi><mn>4</mn><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msubsup></math> respectively are:
solution:
(i) FeCl42- Fe2+ 3d6→4 unpaired electrons.

μ=nn+2BM

=44+2BM

=24 BM⇒4.90 BM

 

(ii) CoC2O43-3 is a strong field ligand & causes
pairing & hence no. unpaired electron

μ=0

 

(iii) MnO42-  Mn+6 it has one unpaired electron.

Mn+6⇒Ar3 d1  μ=nn+2BM

=11+2BM

=3BM⇒1.73 BM

q:
Consider the following reaction and statements:

CoNH34Br2++Br-→CoNH33Br3+NH3

(i) Two isomers are produced if the reactant complex ion is a cis-isomer.

(ii) Two isomers are produced if the reactant complex ion is a trans-isomer.

(iii) Only one isomer is produced if the reactant complex ion is a trans-isomer.

(iv) Only one isomer is produced if the reactant complex ion is a cis-isomer.

The correct statements are
question:
Consider the following reaction and statements:

CoNH34Br2++Br-→CoNH33Br3+NH3

(i) Two isomers are produced if the reactant complex ion is a cis-isomer.

(ii) Two isomers are produced if the reactant complex ion is a trans-isomer.

(iii) Only one isomer is produced if the reactant complex ion is a trans-isomer.

(iv) Only one isomer is produced if the reactant complex ion is a cis-isomer.

The correct statements are
solution:
From the below image it is clear that two isomers are produced if the reactant complexion is a cis-isomer, as well as only one isomer, is produced if the reactant complex is a trans-isomer.

Two isomers are produced if the reactant complex ion is a cis-isomer because the cis isomer can further form facial and meridional isomers.

Only one isomer is produced if the reactant complex ion is a trans-isomer because it cannot form any isomeric isomers.

Trans → only meridonial

Cis → fac and mer

subject: Chemistry
q: Which one of the following complexes will have $\Delta_0=0$ and $\mu=5.96$ B.M.?
question: Which one of the following complexes will have $\Delta_0=0$ and $\mu=5.96$ B.M.?
solution:
(1) $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{3+} \quad \mathrm{Co}^{3+} \Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{~d}^6 4 \mathrm{~s}^0$

$=[-0.4 \times 6+0.6 \times(0)] \Delta_0=-2.4 \Delta_0$
(2)
$\begin{aligned}
& {\left[\mathrm{Mn}(\mathrm{SCN})_6\right]^4} \\
& \mathrm{Mn}^{2+} \Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{~d}^5 4 \mathrm{~s}^0
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mu=\sqrt{35} \text { B.M. }=5.96 \text { B.M. } \\
& \mathrm{CFSE}=(-0.4 \times 3+0.6 \times 2) \Delta_0
\end{aligned}$
So $\Delta_0=0$
(3)
$\begin{aligned}
& {\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{-4} \quad \mathrm{Fe}^{2+} \Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{~d}^6 4 \mathrm{~s}^0}
\end{aligned}$
$\mathrm{CFSE}=-2.4 \Delta_0$
(4) $\left[\mathrm{FeF}_6\right]^{4-}$
$\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} \Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{~d}^6 4 \mathrm{~s}^0$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mu=\sqrt{24} \text { B.M. }=4.89 \text { B.M. } \\
& \text { CFSE }=(-0.4 \times 4+0.6 \times 2) \Delta_0=-1.2 \Delta_0
\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Arrange the following coordination compounds in the increasing order of magnetic moments. (Atomic numbers: Mn=25;Fe=26)

A. FeF63-

B. FeCN63-

C. MnCl63- (high spin)

D. MnCN63-

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Arrange the following coordination compounds in the increasing order of magnetic moments. (Atomic numbers: Mn=25;Fe=26)

A. FeF63-

B. FeCN63-

C. MnCl63- (high spin)

D. MnCN63-

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
solution:
Magnetic moment is directly proportional to the number of unpaired in the central metal atom of a complex.

It is calculated by the formula, μ=n(n+2)BM

(A) FeF63-

Fe+3→3d54s0

n=5

(B) FeCN63-

Fe+3→3d54s0

n=1

(C) MnCl63-

Mn+3→3d44s0

n=4

(D) MnCN63-

Mn+3→3d44s0

n=2

Hence, correct order of magnetic moment is A>C>D>B

q:
Given below are two statements :

Statement I: NiCN42- is square planar and diamagnetic complex, with dsp2 hybridization for Ni but NiCO4 is tetrahedral, paramagnetic and with sp3 hybridication for Ni.

Statement II : NiCl42- and NiCO4 both have same d-electron configuration, have same geometry and are paramagnetic.

In light the above statements, choose the correct answer form the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements :

Statement I: NiCN42- is square planar and diamagnetic complex, with dsp2 hybridization for Ni but NiCO4 is tetrahedral, paramagnetic and with sp3 hybridication for Ni.

Statement II : NiCl42- and NiCO4 both have same d-electron configuration, have same geometry and are paramagnetic.

In light the above statements, choose the correct answer form the options given below
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true.
Statement I is correct but statement II is false.
Statement I is incorrect but statement II is true.
Both Statement I and Statement II are false.
solution:
NiCN42-: d8 configuration, SFL, sq. planar splitting dsp2, diamagnetic.

NiCO4 : d10 config (after excitation), SFL, tetrahedral splitting sp3, diamagnetic.

NiCl42-: d8 config, WFL, tetrahedral splitting sp3, paramagnetic(2 unpaired e-).

Electronic configuration of Ni atom in NiCO4

subject: Chemistry
q: Total number of unpaired electrons present in the central metal atoms/ions of $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_{4}\right],\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_{4}\right]^{2-},\left[\mathrm{PtCl}_{2}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right],\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]^{2-}$ and $\left[\mathrm{Pt}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]^{2-}$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: Total number of unpaired electrons present in the central metal atoms/ions of $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_{4}\right],\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_{4}\right]^{2-},\left[\mathrm{PtCl}_{2}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right],\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]^{2-}$ and $\left[\mathrm{Pt}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]^{2-}$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
$[Ni(CO)_4]$:
Ni is zero-valent with configuration $[Ar]3d^{10}4s^2$.
CO is a strong field ligand causing complete pairing in the filled $3d^{10}$ orbital.
Unpaired electrons = 0.

$[NiCl_4]^{2-}$:
Ni is $+2$ with configuration $[Ar]3d^8$.
Cl is a weak field ligand producing high-spin tetrahedral geometry with configuration $3d^8$: five orbitals with two electrons paired, three electrons unpaired.
Unpaired electrons = 2.

$[PtCl_2(NH_3)_2]$:
Pt is $+2$ with configuration $[Xe]4f^{14}5d^8$.
Pt(II) adopts square planar geometry with strong-field splitting leading to complete pairing: $5d^8$ configuration places all electrons in bonding orbitals.
Unpaired electrons = 0.

$[Ni(CN)_4]^{2-}$:
Ni is $+2$ with $3d^8$ configuration.
CN is a strong field ligand.
Square planar geometry is preferred, resulting in all electrons paired.
Unpaired electrons = 0.

$[Pt(CN)_4]^{2-}$:
Pt is $+2$ with $5d^8$ configuration.
CN is a strong field ligand producing square planar geometry with complete pairing.
Unpaired electrons = 0.

Total unpaired electrons = 0 + 2 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 2.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following complex is homoleptic?
question: Which of the following complex is homoleptic?
solution:
Homoleptic complexes are compounds in which all the ligands bound to the central metal atom or ion are identical.

Homoleptic complexes have only one type of ligand.

Ni(CN)42- has only one type of ligand.

Hence, option A is the answer.

solution: H2O is a weak field ligand. Crystal field stabilisation energy is maximum for d3 and d8 cases with weak field ligands among the 3d-series elements.But CFSE increases from 3d to 5d series elements due to the increase in nuclear charge. Hence, OsH2O63+ complex case, the CFSE is maximum.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: Hybridisation, shape and spin only magnetic moment of $\mathrm{K}_{3}\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{CO}_{3}\right)_{3}\right]$ is $\mathrm{sp}^{3} \mathrm{~d}^{2}$, octahedral and 4.9 BM respectively.
Statement II: Geometry, hybridisation and spin only magnetic moment values $(\mathrm{BM})$ of the ions $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]^{2-},\left[\mathrm{MnBr}_{4}\right]^{2-}$ and $\left[\mathrm{CoF}_{6}\right]^{3-}$ respectively are square planar, tetrahedral, octahedral; $\mathrm{dsp}^{2}, \mathrm{sp}^{3}, \mathrm{sp}^{3} \mathrm{~d}^{2}$ and $0,5.9,4.9$.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: Hybridisation, shape and spin only magnetic moment of $\mathrm{K}_{3}\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{CO}_{3}\right)_{3}\right]$ is $\mathrm{sp}^{3} \mathrm{~d}^{2}$, octahedral and 4.9 BM respectively.
Statement II: Geometry, hybridisation and spin only magnetic moment values $(\mathrm{BM})$ of the ions $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]^{2-},\left[\mathrm{MnBr}_{4}\right]^{2-}$ and $\left[\mathrm{CoF}_{6}\right]^{3-}$ respectively are square planar, tetrahedral, octahedral; $\mathrm{dsp}^{2}, \mathrm{sp}^{3}, \mathrm{sp}^{3} \mathrm{~d}^{2}$ and $0,5.9,4.9$.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
In Statement I, for $K_3[Co(CO_3)_3]$, Cobalt is in $+3$ oxidation state ($Co^{3+}: 3d^6$). Carbonate ($CO_3^{2-}$) is a weak field ligand. Thus, no pairing occurs, resulting in 4 unpaired electrons. Hybridisation is $sp^3d^2$, shape is octahedral, and $\mu = \sqrt{4(4+2)} = \sqrt{24} \approx 4.9$ BM. Statement I is true.
In Statement II:
For $[Ni(CN)_4]^{2-}$, $Ni^{2+}$ is $3d^8$. $CN^-$ is a strong field ligand, causing pairing. Hybridisation is $dsp^2$, geometry is square planar, and $\mu = 0$ BM.
For $[MnBr_4]^{2-}$, $Mn^{2+}$ is $3d^5$. $Br^-$ is a weak field ligand. Hybridisation is $sp^3$, geometry is tetrahedral, and $\mu = \sqrt{5(5+2)} = \sqrt{35} \approx 5.9$ BM.
For $[CoF_6]^{3-}$, $Co^{3+}$ is $3d^6$. $F^-$ is a weak field ligand. Hybridisation is $sp^3d^2$, geometry is octahedral, and $\mu = \sqrt{4(4+2)} = \sqrt{24} \approx 4.9$ BM.
Statement II is true.
Both statements are true.

q: The pair of metal ions that can give a spin only magnetic moment of <math><mn>3.9</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>B</mi><mi>M</mi></math> for the complex <math><mo>[</mo><mi>M</mi><msub><mrow><mo>(</mo><msub><mrow><mi>H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi>O</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></msub><mo>]</mo><mi>C</mi><msub><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> , is:

solution:
If the complex is MH2O6Cl2, M will have +2 oxidation state and hence, it has already lost 2 electrons from s- orbital. Since it has μ=3.9 = n( n+2 ) ⇒n=3

3 unpaired so, the configuration can either be:

subject: Chemistry
q: The IUPAC name of the complex <math><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><msub><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math> is
question: The IUPAC name of the complex <math><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><msub><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math> is
options: Bisammine (methanamine) chlorido platinum <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> chloride
solution:
NH 3  is neutral ligand, its IUPAC name is ammine

Cl  is negative ligand, its IUPAC name is chlrido

NH 2 CH 3  is neutral ligand, its IUPAC name is methanamine

Oxidation state of platinum ⇒ x+2×0+1 −1 +1 0 =1

x=2
Naming of ligand should be done in the alphabetically order.

The IUPAC name of the complex is Diamminechlorido (methanamine) platinum II chloride

subject: Chemistry
q: Complex <math><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>  Of composition <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><msub><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></mrow></msub></math> Has a spin only magnetic moment of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3.83</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>.</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo>.</mo></math> It reacts with <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></math> And shows geometrical isomerism. The IUPAC nomenclature of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> Is:
question: Complex <math><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>  Of composition <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><msub><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></mrow></msub></math> Has a spin only magnetic moment of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3.83</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>.</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo>.</mo></math> It reacts with <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></math> And shows geometrical isomerism. The IUPAC nomenclature of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> Is:
solution:
CrH2O6Clnμcomplexspin=3.83 B.M.
From data of magnetic moment oxidation number of Cr should be +3
Hence, complex is CrH2O6Cl3.

The above complex formula can have following different types of complexes-
1. CrH2O6Cl3 As chlorine atoms are outside of co-ordination sphere hence it can react with Silver nitrate to form silver chloride, but can not show Geometrical Isomerism because all 6 ligands are same i.e. aqua

2. CrH2O5ClCl2.H2O 

This complex can react with silver nitrate as chlorine atoms are outside of co-ordination sphere, but can not show Geometrical Isomerism.

3. CrH2O4Cl2Cl.2H2O

This complex can react with silver nitrate as one chlorine atom is outside of co-ordination sphere and also show Geometrical isomerism. The IUPAC name of this complex is Tetraaquadichloridochromium(III) chloride dihydrate.

4. CrH2O3Cl33H2O

This complex can not react with silver nitrate because not chlorine atom is outside of co-ordination sphere, but this complex can show Geometrical isomerism(Facial and meridional ).

subject: Chemistry
q:
$5.33$ g of $CrCl_3 \cdot 6H_2O$, which is a $1 : 3$ electrolyte, is dissolved in water and is passed through a cation exchanger. The chloride ions in the eluted solution, on treatment with $AgNO_3$ results in $8.61$ g of $AgCl$. The ratio of moles of complex reacted and moles of $AgCl$ formed is _______ $\times 10^{-2}$. (Nearest integer)
[Molar mass in g mol$^{-1}$ Cr : $52$, Ag : $108$, Cl : $35.5$, H : $1$, O : $16$]
question:
$5.33$ g of $CrCl_3 \cdot 6H_2O$, which is a $1 : 3$ electrolyte, is dissolved in water and is passed through a cation exchanger. The chloride ions in the eluted solution, on treatment with $AgNO_3$ results in $8.61$ g of $AgCl$. The ratio of moles of complex reacted and moles of $AgCl$ formed is _______ $\times 10^{-2}$. (Nearest integer)
[Molar mass in g mol$^{-1}$ Cr : $52$, Ag : $108$, Cl : $35.5$, H : $1$, O : $16$]
solution:
Molar mass of $CrCl_3 \cdot 6H_2O = 52 + 3 \times 35.5 + 6 \times 18 = 266.5 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$

Moles of complex reacted $= \dfrac{5.33}{266.5} = 0.02 \text{ mol}$

Since the complex is a $1:3$ electrolyte, it dissociates to give one complex cation and three chloride anions. Its formula is $[Cr(H_2O)_6]Cl_3$.

When passed through a cation exchanger, the complex cation is exchanged, and the chloride ions pass into the eluted solution.

Molar mass of $AgCl = 108 + 35.5 = 143.5 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$

Moles of $AgCl$ formed $= \dfrac{8.61}{143.5} = 0.06 \text{ mol}$

The ratio of moles of complex reacted to moles of $AgCl$ formed is:

$\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{0.02}{0.06} = \dfrac{1}{3} = 0.3333...$

This can be written as $33.33 \times 10^{-2}$.

Rounding to the nearest integer, we get $33$.

Answer: $33$

q:
Number of Complexes with even number of electrons in \(t_{2 g}\) orbitals is -
\(\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+},\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+},\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{3+},\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+},\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}\)
question:
Number of Complexes with even number of electrons in \(t_{2 g}\) orbitals is -
\(\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+},\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+},\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{3+},\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+},\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}\)

subject: Chemistry
q:
Number of complexes from the following with even number of unpaired " \(\mathrm{d}\) " electrons is \(\qquad\) \(\left[\mathrm{V}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{3+},\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+},\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{3+},\left[\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{3+},\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}\)
[Given atomic numbers : \(\mathrm{V}=23, \mathrm{Cr}=24, \mathrm{Fe}=26, \mathrm{Ni}=28 \mathrm{Cu}=29\)]
question:
Number of complexes from the following with even number of unpaired " \(\mathrm{d}\) " electrons is \(\qquad\) \(\left[\mathrm{V}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{3+},\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+},\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{3+},\left[\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{3+},\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}\)
[Given atomic numbers : \(\mathrm{V}=23, \mathrm{Cr}=24, \mathrm{Fe}=26, \mathrm{Ni}=28 \mathrm{Cu}=29\)]
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & {\left[\mathrm{V}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{3+} \rightarrow \mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{sp}^3} \\ & { }_{23} \mathrm{~V}:-[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^3 4 \mathrm{~s}^2 \\ & \left.\mathrm{~V}^{+3}:-[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^2, \mathrm{n}=2 \text { (even number of unpaired } \mathrm{e}^{-}\right) \\ & {\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+} \rightarrow \mathrm{sp}^3 \mathrm{~d}^2} \\ & { }_{24} \mathrm{Cr}:-[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^5 4 \mathrm{~s}^1 \\ & \left.\mathrm{Cr}^{+2}:-[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^4, \mathrm{n}=4 \text { (even number of unpaired } \mathrm{e}^{-}\right)\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & {\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{3+} \rightarrow \mathrm{sp}^3 \mathrm{~d}^2} \\ & \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}:-[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^5 4 \mathrm{~s}^0 \\ & \mathrm{n}=5\left(\text { odd number of unpaired } \mathrm{e}^{-}\right) \\ & {\left[\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{3+} \rightarrow \mathrm{sp}^3 \mathrm{~d}^2} \\ & \mathrm{Ni}:-[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^8 4 \mathrm{~s}^2 \\ & \left.\mathrm{Ni}^{+3}:-[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^7, \mathrm{n}=3 \text { (odd number of unpaired } \mathrm{e}^{-}\right) \\ & {\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+} \rightarrow \mathrm{sp}^3 \mathrm{~d}^2} \\ & \mathrm{Cu}:-[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^9 4 \mathrm{~s}^0 \\ & \left.\mathrm{n}=1 \text { (odd number of unpaired } \mathrm{e}^{-}\right)\end{aligned}$

q:
Among the statements a-d , the incorrect ones are:
a Octahedral CoIII complexes with strong field ligands have very high magnetic moments
b When ∆0<P , the d-electron configuration of CoIII in an octahedral complex is teg4eg2
c Wavelength of light absorbed by Coen33+ is lower than that of CoF63-
d If the ∆0 for an octahedral complex of CoIII is 18,000cm-1, the ∆t for its tetrahedral complex with the same ligand will be 16,000cm-1.
question:
Among the statements a-d , the incorrect ones are:
a Octahedral CoIII complexes with strong field ligands have very high magnetic moments
b When ∆0<P , the d-electron configuration of CoIII in an octahedral complex is teg4eg2
c Wavelength of light absorbed by Coen33+ is lower than that of CoF63-
d If the ∆0 for an octahedral complex of CoIII is 18,000cm-1, the ∆t for its tetrahedral complex with the same ligand will be 16,000cm-1.
solution:
So if there are strong field ligands in complex, pairing of electrons will take place, due to which number of unpaired electrons decrease due to which magnetic moment decrease.
(c) Splitting power of ethylenediamine (en) is greater than fluoride F − ligand therefore more energy absorbed by Co en 3+   as compared to  CoF 6 3−

So wave length of light absorbed by Co en 3+ is lower than that of CoF 6 3−

(d) Δt=49Δ0

so if  Δ0=18,000 cm−1

Δt=49×18000=8000 cm−1

Statement (a) and (d) are incorrect.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following complex will show largest splitting of d-orbitals?
question: Which of the following complex will show largest splitting of d-orbitals?
solution:
Some ligands are able to produce strong fields in which case, the splitting will be large whereas others produce weak fields and consequently result in a small splitting of d orbitals.

In general, ligands can be arranged in a series called spectrochemical series according to which maximum splitting is done by the strongest ligand which is cyanide (CN-) ion in this case.
